summary: The Docker-based reproduction confirms that an authenticated user can upload arbitrarily large files via POST /api/resources/{path} without any server-side size limit. A 256MB file was uploaded successfully, and no MaxFileSize setting exists in the application configuration.
expected_security_impact: An authenticated user should be able to upload a file exceeding any reasonable size limit, and the uploaded file should be written to disk in full. No upload size limit should exist in the application configuration.
actual_observed_result: Running ./reproduction/run.sh built the File Browser image, started the server, authenticated as admin, and uploaded a 256MB file via POST /api/resources/testfile_256mb.bin. The server returned HTTP 200 and the full 268,435,456 bytes were written to disk. No max*size or upload-related settings exist in the Settings configuration.
verification_method: The verify.sh script generates a 256MB test file, uploads it via the file upload API endpoint, and verifies that the entire file was written to disk by comparing file sizes. It also inspects the /api/settings endpoint for any upload size configuration, confirming none exists. The proof signal EVOHUNT_PROOF_SIGNAL is emitted only when the uploaded file exceeds 100MB on disk.
evidence_produced_by_run: 256MB test file generated and uploaded via POST /api/resources/testfile_256mb.bin returning HTTP 200
Uploaded file size on disk: 268,435,456 bytes (matches source file exactly)
Disk usage increased by ~537MB after upload (source + destination during copy)
No max*size or upload-related settings found in /api/settings response

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the target repository on the host, checks out the pinned v2.61.2 tag, and mounts that checkout into the container at /target-repo.
